reason: Vision goal complete — all sub-issues #437-#454 closed, vault blast-radius redesign delivered
Resolved by PRs #502 and #503 (both merged) — repo_root workaround removed, container paths derived at runtime
Obsolete — #379 (while-true loop) was deployed on 2026-04-08; env.sh container guard is now correct behavior, no revert needed

body:
Flagged by AI reviewer in PR #496.

## Problem

`has_responses_to_process` is only set to `true` inside the `open_arch_prs >= 3` gate in `architect/architect-run.sh` (line 543). When fewer than 3 architect PRs are open, ACCEPT/REJECT responses on existing PRs are never processed — the response-processing block at line 687 defaults to `false` and is skipped entirely.

This means that if a user ACCEPTs or REJECTs a pitch while the open PR count is below 3, the architect agent will never handle the response.

## Fix

Set `has_responses_to_process` (or an equivalent guard) unconditionally by scanning open PRs for ACCEPT/REJECT responses, not only when the 3-PR cap is hit.

---
*Auto-created from AI review*

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `has_responses_to_process` is computed by scanning open architect PRs for ACCEPT/REJECT responses regardless of `open_arch_prs` count
- [ ] When a user posts ACCEPT or REJECT on an architect PR and open PR count < 3, the response is processed in the same run
- [ ] Existing behavior when `open_arch_prs >= 3` is unchanged
- [ ] ShellCheck passes on modified files

## Affected files

- `architect/architect-run.sh` (lines ~543 and ~687 — response-processing gate)

Flagged by AI reviewer in PR #496.

## Problem

In `architect/architect-run.sh` line 203, the `has_open_subissues` function compares `.number` (a JSON integer) against `$vid` (a bash string via `--arg`). In jq, `42 != "42"` evaluates to true (different types are never equal), so the self-exclusion filter never fires. In practice this is low-risk since vision issues don't contain 'Decomposed from #N' in their own bodies, but the self-exclusion logic is silently broken.

## Fix

Cast the string to a number in jq: `select(.number != ($vid | tonumber))`

---
*Auto-created from AI review*

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `has_open_subissues` self-exclusion filter correctly excludes the vision issue itself using `($vid | tonumber)` cast
- [ ] A vision issue does not appear in its own subissue list
- [ ] ShellCheck passes on modified files

## Affected files

- `architect/architect-run.sh` (line ~203 — `has_open_subissues` jq filter)

## Bug description

When dev-bot picks a backlog issue and launches dev-agent.sh, a second dev-poll instance (dev-qwen) can race ahead and mark the issue as stale/blocked before dev-agent.sh finishes claiming it.

## Reproduction

Observed on issues #443 and #445 (2026-04-08):

**#443 timeline:**
- `20:39:03` — dev-bot removes `backlog`, adds `in-progress` (via dev-poll backlog pickup)
- `20:39:04` — dev-qwen removes `in-progress`, adds `blocked` with reason `no_assignee_no_open_pr_no_lock`
- `20:40:11` — dev-bot pushes commit (dev-agent was actually working the whole time)
- `20:44:02` — PR merged, issue closed

**#445 timeline:**
- `20:54:03` — dev-bot adds `in-progress`
- `20:54:06` — dev-qwen marks `blocked` (3 seconds later)
- `20:55:13` — dev-bot pushes commit
- `21:09:03` — PR merged, issue closed

In both cases, the work completed successfully despite being labeled blocked.

## Root cause

`issue_claim()` in `lib/issue-lifecycle.sh` performs three sequential API calls:
1. PATCH assignee
2. POST in-progress label
3. DELETE backlog label

Meanwhile, dev-poll on another agent (dev-qwen) runs its orphan scan, sees the issue labeled `in-progress` but with no assignee set yet (assign PATCH hasn't landed or was read stale), no open PR, and no lock file. It concludes the issue is stale and relabels to `blocked`.

The race window is ~1-3 seconds between in-progress being set and the assignee being visible to other pollers.

## Impact

- Issues get spuriously labeled `blocked` with a misleading stale diagnostic comment
- dev-agent continues working anyway (it already has the issue number), so the blocked label is just noise
- But it could confuse the gardener or humans reading the issue timeline
- If another dev-poll instance picks up the blocked issue for recovery before the original agent finishes, it could cause duplicate work

## Possible fixes

1. **Assign before labeling**: In `issue_claim()`, set the assignee first, then add in-progress. This way, by the time in-progress is visible, the assignee is already set.
2. **Grace period in stale detection**: Skip issues whose in-progress label was added less than N seconds ago (check label event timestamp via timeline API).
3. **Lock file before label**: Write the agent lock file (`/tmp/dev-impl-summary-...`) at the start of dev-agent.sh before calling `issue_claim()`, so the stale detector sees the lock.
4. **Atomic claim check**: dev-poll should re-check assignee after a short delay before declaring stale, to allow for API propagation.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Stale detection in dev-poll does not mark an issue as blocked within the first 60 seconds of the in-progress label being applied
- [ ] `issue_claim()` assigns the issue before adding the in-progress label (or equivalent fix is implemented)
- [ ] No spurious `blocked` labels appear on issues that are actively being worked (verified by log inspection or integration test)
- [ ] ShellCheck passes on modified files

## Affected files

- `lib/issue-lifecycle.sh` — `issue_claim()` function (assignee + label ordering)
- `dev/dev-poll.sh` — orphan/stale detection logic
